author: @achmyta0160
text:
Stainless steel consists of part chromium. Chromium oxidizes to chromium oxides during electrolysis. One of those oxides is chromium oxide VI ( hexavalent ).
HIGHLY CANCEROUS. 
In civilized countries, you can't by law ( and shouldn't regardless ) just dispose of that "rust". There is special protocol for disposing it(!).
Don't breathe it in. Don't dump it outside/down the drain. Don't touch it with bare hands. 
Don't use stainless steel as a waste electrode.

USE GRAPHITE RODS/PLATES. Best conductivity, best porousness, cheapest, most efficient. Breaks down into graphite powder/graphene.

Robert, a future video idea might be to put a cheap submersible pump in the electrolyzer. Hydrogen bubbles cling to the electrodes acting as an insulator reducing efficiency, vibrating the plates can free hydrogen and a pumped solution flowing over the plates from the bottom up could increase efficiency too. Eliminate the bubbles to increase efficiency and yield. Is there a best sonic or ultrasonic frequency for breaking the hydrogen bubbles free of the surface?

How do you separate out the oxygen from the gas stream? A cheap plastic or glass tube open at both ends would work to do this, silicone it to the inside spacers and a rubber seal at the top to the lid and put the O2 collection tube lower in the gas column.

author: @___Chris___
text:
Several people are commenting here that Rob is violating the 1st law of thermodynamics with his remarks in this video. At a closer look, this isn't really the case. I was confused for a second, too, but I assume it was only based on a misunderstanding or maybe not the best choice of words. What he didn't say is that he was only looking at the VOLTAGE he put in. In this sense it is correct, but it's not the complete picture. The discrepancy comes from the fact that not the entire chemically bound energy was considered in that equation. Urea electrolysis doesn't create real "Brown's Gas", i.e. no stoichiometric mixture of hydrogen and oxygen. There will be H2 produced at the cathode and N2 and CO2 at the anode. Urea will be consumed over time as kind of a 'fuel'. There's also some energy saved because to make it burn you would be required to take oxygen e.g. from atmospheric air, which is abundant and basically 'free'. All things considered, i.e. WITH the urea consumption and the external oxygen supply, the net energetic result is of course NOT positive. If anything is supposed to be 'free' here, it's only in the sense that oxygen is all around us and that urea is incredibly cheap. The laws of physics still apply. There's nothing free in a strict thermodynamical sense and when viewed as a closed system - and I'm 100% sure that Rob would agree to that. The thing is: we won't run out of oxygen or urea any time soon (the latter is literally in urine!), so you can consider the system as partially open.

On a side-note: there is no such thing as a perfect electrode. The required voltage is always a little higher than the theoretical voltage according to the Nernst equation. This is also the case for the 0.37V that he mentioned. The exact amount of required "overvoltage" varies between electrode materials (graphite and iron are a bit at the lower end, but for many materials the electrolysis overvoltage is even beyond 1.0V!). For the complete equation, this overvoltage (and a bit of heat loss) must be taken into account and I have no doubts that Rob is aware of that.

author: @petec6690
text:
7 minutes 42 seconds - "there you go, it's generating lots of gas."  Sorry, I didn't see it generating any gas at all.  I still have to watch the full video.  At 9 min's 30 sec's. you claim to get more energy out than you put in.  Physics 101 - you can't get more energy out than you put in.  If you put in 100 watts of energy you are (in a perfect  world) going to get 100 watts out.  Not 110 watts, not 200 watts, just not possible to get more energy out than you put in.  And since this isn't a perfect world, what I think you're confusing is that with water you may get, and I'm going to make up some numbers here, you may get 87% of the energy out, whereas - and I don't know this to be true - with urea you may get 92%.  It's an improvement (maybe), but you'll never exceed 100% out.  Just not possible to create energy.  You can convert it from one form to another, but there are almost always losses.  Converting gasoline (energy) into heat - you get the same amount of energy as you started with.  Some of it is lost to friction, some of it is lost as heat.  But you can't get more energy out than you put in.  Gasoline has high energy density.  That's why it's superior to electric cars; you don't have to carry tons of batteries to get the same range as a few pounds of fuel.

And I never saw any gas being produced.  One way to prove gas production is to dip the end of the tube into a jar of water.  That way we can see the bubbles as the gas escapes the tube.

author: @fishyerik
text:
No, you are not getting net positive energy out from the process. The urea represents energy input in that equation when you calculate the energy output based on the potential energy relative to the binding of hydrogen and oxygen into water.
That is, at 0.37V you're not splitting water, but the urea. When you run out of urea the voltage needed to continue to produce hydrogen will rise to the voltage needed to split water.

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, in the exact same way, the urea is a fuel of sorts in this situation, energy input, and has actually been evaluated as a fuel, or "hydrogen carrier", as hydrogen in itself is absurdly impractical to deal with in relevant quantities. But still, your process is net positive in the same sense as in burning fuel in a car, that typically average somewhere around 20% net efficiency from input to useful energy.

text: You forgot to mention the discolouring of the solution is mainly due to the decomposing stainless steel - in my understanding if left for a considerable length of time, you'll start seeing a very noticeable build up of the materials used to make the s/steel deposit in the solution eg carbon and iron ore. You can demonstrate the s/steel has decomposed simply by removing the plates and then place a magnet on the side of the glass and watch the iron in the solution be attracted to it. The gas products therefore largely originate from the metal as we have to take into account a small amount of urea was added and that too would have decomposed! Water decomposes the metal/urea and the electricity is the true catalyst speeding up rate of decomposition!

replies.author: @jakegarrett8109
replies.text:
So just shorting power supplies together? If you’re using for example a 3v solar panel but then using 12v computer power supply the solar panel then does nothing (hopefully it doesn’t break, but assuming it doesn’t, it contributes no power), then if you added say a third power supply like a 24v power supply you’ll just be frying the 12v power supply (if by some miracle it doesn’t fry it still has less voltage potential so it’s just not going to power it). The only way it works is if they are all similar voltage potential (and don’t hook up a 12v lithium battery to a 12v power supply either, because that will still explode). Basically it’s useless unless you are using for example maybe 12v windmill and 12v solar for the example of it sometimes being sunny or windy.

Voltage is like water in a pipe, the one with the most potential (height of water in the tube or voltage) is the one that is used, only when the water in the tubes are level (same voltage) do they both contribute. In the case of low powered solar at high voltage it could contribute a low amount of current in parallel as it’s voltage drops to match the power supply (example is a 19v solar panel dropping to 12v when in parallel with the power supply and providing its little bit of current).

You can use the power from peltier thermoelectric generator, TEG, powered with waste heat to make the alternator take less power from the engine, that would reduce fuel consumption more, and it is a lot less complicated and less dangerous. Also it would make the battery last longer as lead acid batteries last longer if they are not discharged. 
It would not make a huge difference though, TEGs are not very efficient, and it is difficult to harvest waste heat very efficiently, but reducing the power the alternator draws would be much better use of what small amounts of electric energy you can get from TEGs in a car than taking the detour of hydrogen production.

replies.author: @fishyerik
replies.text:
@Mr Brightside The energy lost to breaking is typically few percent of the total energy used, compressed air does not do much for efficiency, it can increase power output, but with increased fuel consumption.  Compressing a lot of air during a short time will mostly produce heat, very small part of the used energy will be stored as compressed air. Compressed air also has limited value in a car. All in all, that system would "pay", in form of gained efficiency, for it's own weight. 

Internal combustion engines in cars produces a lot more heat than usable energy. Granted, it is not easy to collect a very large part of that energy, and turn it in to electricity, but a TEG for a few dollars can produce a few watts as long as it is kept hot on one side, and "cool" on the other. They are not big, not heavy, not overly expensive, so you can use multiple TEGs, as long as you can cool the cool side properly.

author: @ianmoone2359
text:
Serious question please.
Here downunder 🇦🇺 where it’s hotter than hades and we get so much sunshine that it shines at night time 😉😜😜😂- everyone is installing solar power & selling surplus power into the grid during the day time when no one is home to utilise it.
At night time when the sun has set & the family are all home from work / school & chew up copious power, for lights, cooking & infotainment, we buy that same power back from the grid at 4 times the electric Tarif we got for the power we put into the grid during the daytime when no one was home to utilise it.
So the “answer” is Tesla “powerwall” lithium ion batteries to store your own power generated during the day time & draw down on it at night time rather than buy power from the grid at 4 times what you sold it for during the day when you had surplus.
The “problem” being that the “Powerwall” li-ion batteries, cost ~$10k each and an average family home needs 2!

It takes forever to see a return on that 20k investment.

Is there any good reason that solar power during the day couldn’t electrolyse water into Hydrogen & Oxygen & store (compress) it into storage tanks on a household level, then at night time - re combine the DiHydrogenMonoxode back into water byproduct - to generate the power we need in our homes during darkness hours?

Might not it be cheaper to be doing this every day than charging li-ion battery’s during the day & discharging them at night?

It Tesla Powerwall battery charging / discharging MORE or LESS efficient than electrolysing urea solution, then re-combusting it to generate home power?

To me it seems like a no brainer, to electrolyse water or urea solution and compress it into storage tanks during the day & use it again at night than charge / discharge Li-ion batteries?

I’m guessing the engineers at Tesla are smart enough to have investigated this and passed it up for logical reasons (risk of hydrogen bomb explosions for starters).?

author: @yougeo
text:
A very good and inexpensive way! And the urea trick is the masterpiece. That's really something.

 Two other easy ways to make elctrolysers are to use blank precut squarish stainlesssteel wall covers for light switches ($17 US for 10 on Amazon) https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ERJVbJ4r75A


or use stainless steel pipes one sized inside the other with a plastic zip tie used as separators. Also according to all builders of these things you actually need to let the stainless device run overnight to somehow condition the plates. Don't know why that's true but virtually all makers of these devices say the output increases substantially after a break in period.


Now you can try the Stan Meyer circuit which uses distilled water and actually makes the capacitor water tank part of a radio resonant tank circuit with a inductance coil and ...most importantly and often forgotten.. a diode so the voltage builds up and up between the plates until the water molecule is more efficiently bounced apart rather than ripped apart with lots of amps. 



here is the circuit. notice the electrolyzer filled with insulating distilled water IS the capacitor.
https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/7e/73/28/f1263c3748ae72/US4936961-drawings-page-3.png


supposedly uses a lot lot less amps. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QxIiRrl6l6E  text about it  http://jnaudin.free.fr/wfcbooster/indexen.htm ) by the Way Jean-Louis Naudin is an exceptional and meticulous experimenter who documents his experiments and tests in great detail.)

in that test Jean uses a high current simple source that produces hho from pure water but with too much current.

here he replicates Meyers actual patent and produces the same amount of hho gas with an incredibly small amount of current and voltage http://jnaudin.free.fr/wfcbooster/WFCbooster05en.htm  video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4nnvley2N4g
(Wish I had a two function generator waveform machine because i would love to duplicate this.)

 most people who make meyer circuit replications on youtube dont use the diode and add electrolyte to the water which makes it NOT a meyer circuit but a simple electrolyzer. putting electrolyte int he water destroys the meyer circuit because it depends on the voltage between plates building and building without current passing through the water until the molecular bound between the h and the o2 is broken by the rising voltage bounce.


(by the way for those who say the meyer circuit was proven fraud...no the court ruling was about a dispute about an investor and how payments were to be made and nothing at all to do with the technical competence of the circuit.)

author: @delawarecop
text:
SURFACE AREA is the key to improved productivity and there is no better SURFACE ARE to VOLUME ration than inexpensive Extruded Carbon Water Filter Cartridges from your local hardware store, and using a very inexpensive brush-less DC water Pump to circulate the UREA through the Cartridges and back to a Recovery tank, where the Gases can be easily extracted and used for combustion or whatever else you desire. Obviously you use a Twin Filter Cartridge Housing so that both Carbon Filters act as Anode & Cathode and linked in the center via the common urea feed from the pump.

The efficiency can be further improved by electroplating the Carbon Filters with very inexpensive NICKEL, which acts as a catalyst to the H2 production.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
My guess is that you were not getting many amps.  Surface area is immensely important, additionally a strongly conductive electrolyte helps a lot too. you should use KOH. Unless you have a lot of it, a stainless steel cable does not really have a lot of surface area. and you should never over drive it at 0.5 amps per sq inch of anode, or cathode.  Build an electrolysis cell with 30 sq inches per electrode and drive it at 15 amps and you'll see reasonably good HHO production. or build a series of cells and make the electrolyte more conductive  so that you can still drive it at 15 amps and you have reasonably good production while using less power.
Because Hydrogen has a faster flame front, it helps burn more of the primary fuel  during the power stroke which helps makes a low efficient  engine more efficient. thus improving overall economy despite the power drawn for electrolysis.

replies.author: @reincarnatethylacine70
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 There are a myriad of reasons that HHO injection is a counter productive waste of time. Just a few off the top of my head: There is no free lunch, all the energy to disassociate the water comes from the alternator which is in turn powered by the burning of fuel in the engine. Hydrogen is much less dense than air and actually displaces available oxygen, reducing not adding to power. The IC engine is at best 30% efficient meaning that at the absolute best it is only possible to recover 30% of the energy used in HHO production. On a practical level the volumes of HHO produced by most automotive applications is utterly insignificant in relation to the volume of gasses passing through the motor. The majority of the 'snake oil kits' produce less than 10 litres an hour on the other hand a 2 litre car engine operating at 2500RPM processes 300,000 litres an hour. If you want to produce more HHO you need to burn more fuel, there's no other way.

replies.author: @sceptic33
replies.text: You don't have to pull power from the alternator. Most of the energy released in an engine is wasted as heat. This waste heat can be used to generate electricity using a Stirling engine or peltier module array... No  extra strain on the engine...pure efficiency boost. Simple.

author: @gr1f1th
text: very cool rob.  The biggest complaint I've seen on urea electrolysis is the foaming that occurs.  I don't think 60 watts is a rate that can produce on demand gas production for jeweler torches (most active users of home built electrolysis) So it's probably way less of an issue. The other issue at low volume will be storing it safely. Flashback arrestor and bubbler to some sort of bladder?

replies.author: @kevinroberts781
replies.text: Separate the oxygen and hydrogen by isolating the positive and negative sides. That way no oxygen mixes with the hydrogen. Much safer. Then transfer the hydrogen to a bladder or use a compressor.

author: @YangSword9x
text:
Reason why water is brown is
-iron in stainless steel is rusting...& acid in urea is catallyst.
(Stainless steel is either mixed/OR coated with nickel or other non-rusting metal....problem is coating's cheaper but  doesnt work once surface is cracked/broken/sawed off/wears off, exposes iron to water rusting in steel:carbon/iron mix)

Same thing happened to my $2,000 hydrogen generator....i sent email to supplier & exchanged the steel ones for titanium...
--that was like 10 years ago...now i read i need medical grade titamium rods to prevent it from eroding away tid bits into my car engine block.
(Felt like supercharger/pushed me into my seat on higheay testing)

-always a pleasure/nice to hear advances in technology/& breakthroughs from ROBERT MURRAY!  XD

replies.author: @aarondonaldson4164
replies.text: You cannot store Brown's gas. The oxidizer and the Hydrogen are never separate so the combustion mixture is always stoichiometric. That's why Brown's gas POPs really loudly, and Hydrogen merely whooshes. It would be suicidal to store brown's gas under any kind of pressure exceeding a balloon.
